coredata.dc:title: Experimentally-verified thermal-electrochemical simulations of a cylindrical battery using physics-based, simplified and generalised lumped models
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Sarmadian, Alireza
Widanage, W. Dhammika
Shollock, Barbara
Restuccia, Francesco
coredata.dc:description: The parameterisation for fully physics-based, simplified, and semi-empirical electrochemical-thermal coupled models for prediction of the core temperature of a cylindrical 21,700 cell are addressed in this paper for the first time. When compared to fully physics-based models, the semi-empirical approaches require fewer input parameters overall, simplifying the necessary experimental values and reducing the burden on the battery management system. There are roughly 40 and 30 parameters needed for a Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) and a single particle, respectively. The generalised lumped electrochemical-thermal model, in contrast, requires ten input and three fitting parameters. The present work, aimed at thermal management challenges, couples these electrochemical models with a 2D-axisymmetric heat transfer model to compare qualities such as computational time, accuracy, generalisation, high fidelity, and experimental parameterisations required. The core temperature and voltage predictions for a 21,700 NMC/Si-Graphite cell has been made using coupled thermal-electrochemical models that are accurate (voltage and core temperature standard deviations: 38-130 mV and 0.24–0.79 K) and have a quick response (computation time: 18–262 s). The electrochemical and thermal parameterisation for the models were conducted using the experimental measurements at 1C, 0.3, and 0.7C-rates. Measurements are the core temperature made with a fiber optic sensing system and voltage curves using a battery cycler. For the single particle and P2D models, electrode diffusivities (both negative and positive) are adjusted to calibrate the models. For the single particle model, the results revealed that the core temperature and voltage standard deviations were 0.6 K and 0.13 V, respectively. Depending on the number of mesh, computational times were between 16 and 262 s. For the P2D model, the core temperature and cell voltage standard deviations were found to be 0.038 V and 0.7 K, respectively. Each simulation takes between 27 and 52 s. This is an increase of 189 % over the time required to compute a previously developed lumped model, for which the voltage and core temperature had 0.12 V and 0.8 K standard deviations. P2D simulations in the literature take between 21 and 43 times longer than simulations in the current P2D model. For the generalised lumped model, only the 1C-rate discharge results are used in the calibration, and a generalisation test has been conducted by using a full discharge-charge curve for the first time. The results showed that the change in standard deviations of core temperature (within 21 %) and voltage (within 31 %) are acceptable when a discharge-calibrated model used for full-cycle validations.

The electrochemical- thermal model (schematic view), in which the electrochemical models provide the cell thermal source to the thermal model (2D). The thermal model returns the obtained temperature to the electrochemical model.

head.ce:title: Experimentally-verified thermal-electrochemical simulations of a cylindrical battery using physics-based, simplified and generalised lumped models

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The parameterisation for fully physics-based, simplified, and semi-empirical electrochemical-thermal coupled models for prediction of the core temperature of a cylindrical 21,700 cell are addressed in this paper for the first time. When compared to fully physics-based models, the semi-empirical approaches require fewer input parameters overall, simplifying the necessary experimental values and reducing the burden on the battery management system. There are roughly 40 and 30 parameters needed for a Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) and a single particle, respectively. The generalised lumped electrochemical-thermal model, in contrast, requires ten input and three fitting parameters. The present work, aimed at thermal management challenges, couples these electrochemical models with a 2D-axisymmetric heat transfer model to compare qualities such as computational time, accuracy, generalisation, high fidelity, and experimental parameterisations required. The core temperature and voltage predictions for a 21,700 NMC/Si-Graphite cell has been made using coupled thermal-electrochemical models that are accurate (voltage and core temperature standard deviations: 38-130 mV and 0.24–0.79 K) and have a quick response (computation time: 18–262 s).

The electrochemical and thermal parameterisation for the models were conducted using the experimental measurements at 1C, 0.3, and 0.7C-rates. Measurements are the core temperature made with a fiber optic sensing system and voltage curves using a battery cycler. For the single particle and P2D models, electrode diffusivities (both negative and positive) are adjusted to calibrate the models. For the single particle model, the results revealed that the core temperature and voltage standard deviations were 0.6 K and 0.13 V, respectively. Depending on the number of mesh, computational times were between 16 and 262 s. For the P2D model, the core temperature and cell voltage standard deviations were found to be 0.038 V and 0.7 K, respectively. Each simulation takes between 27 and 52 s. This is an increase of 189 % over the time required to compute a previously developed lumped model, for which the voltage and core temperature had 0.12 V and 0.8 K standard deviations. P2D simulations in the literature take between 21 and 43 times longer than simulations in the current P2D model. For the generalised lumped model, only the 1C-rate discharge results are used in the calibration, and a generalisation test has been conducted by using a full discharge-charge curve for the first time. The results showed that the change in standard deviations of core temperature (within 21 %) and voltage (within 31 %) are acceptable when a discharge-calibrated model used for full-cycle validations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries, largely owing to their cost and energy density with respect to other energy storage devices, have been used as energy sources for a variety of electric devices. The market volume for batteries in the transport sector, namely for electric or hybrid vehicles, is rapidly growing in recent years. In many applications, the heat transfer properties of li-ion batteries during a full cycle (i.e., discharge and charge) are crucial. Battery pack thermal management [1], ageing [2], degradation [3], and design of battery management system [4] are essential in aerospace and automotive applications, for instance. Battery safety, reliability, and longevity [5] are significantly influenced by an electrochemical-thermal model with prediction capability of core temperature instead of only surface temperature simulations as in existing models [6,7]. Low-cost computational modelling is crucial for control strategies and monitoring safety.

An electrochemical-thermal model uses an electrochemical model to simulate the kinetics associated with electrochemical and quantify the input thermal sources required for the coupled heat transfer model. The heat transfer model then computes the temperature distributed in battery for the given physical parameters and boundary conditions. Currently, three categories of lithium-ion models exist; (i) fully physics-based [8], (ii) physics-based with a simplified approach [9], and (iii) equivalent circuit (EC) [10,11] models (as shown in Fig. 1 ). The EC approach functions as a black-box model by using a number of resistances and capacitors to build a dynamic voltage circuit that resemble voltage properties of an active lithium-ion cell. There are also other battery black-box modelling methods, like neural networks [12]. Combining components from various approaches can result in hybrid methods [13].

Newman, Doyle and Fuller created the P2D model [14], which describes the a battery behaviour with porous electrodes. It is a well-known and common physics-based model in the battery modelling community. P2D refers to pseudo two dimensional that is the term to describe this concept, because it assumes that there is a spherical (or cylindrical) particle representing the active material at each location of the electrode, resulting in an electrode thickness dimension and a particle radial dimension. Theory of concentrated solution was considered to define mass transfer in the electrolyte within the porous electrode. The P2D model characteristics is able to reveal the cell internal state while discharging and charging, making it ideal for continuum and macro to microscopic research. The effectiveness of this model for design, optimisation [15], and performance prediction [16] has been demonstrated via significant study.

All the thermodynamic, geometric, kinetic, and physical characteristics of the cell components must be given as inputs to the DFN model. Only via testing can the characteristics of electrode, electrolyte, and separator materials be fully understood. The parameters accuracy affects the reliability of modelling. Each cell design has unique parameters, and not all parameter values are transferrable between cell designs [17]. It is not possible to make accurate predictions from literature-based experimental data, if they come from different sources, there may be differences. Thus, choosing a suitable parameter set to determine a battery is one of the most challenging aspects of physics-based modelling. Around 40 parameters are required for the DFN model to allow simulation.

A model developed by Haran et al. [18] as Single Particle (SP) is a common simplification technique for the P2D approach. The SP model allows an active particle to represent the entire electrode by disregarding the non-uniform lithium ions distribution in the electrolyte. Therefore, the effects of current distribution within the porous electrodes and variations in electrolyte concentration are not considered. The SP model is more computationally efficient than the P2D model with fewer parameters equations (though there are about twenty parameters). As a result, it has developed into a model suitable for systems that operate in real time. The SP model, however, loses precision as a result of the electrochemical process' simplification and can only be used under discharge and charge conditions with low to moderate C-rates [19]. Because there are still >20 input parameters needed, setting up the SP model can be challenging.

A lumped model was recommended by Ekström et al. ([20]) to simplify the battery management system (BMS) parameterisation. Cell potential can be precisely estimated using their method, with three cell overpotential semi-empirical models. The model consists of three elements: a resistive (linear), a kinetic (nonlinear), and a time-dependent element, the latter of which describes the diffusion process in the electrodes. This model has about 10 input parameters. They developed an isothermal model, it should be noted. To obtain transient heat transfer properties, one can combine this electrochemical model with a heat transfer model.

In a review by Alipour et al. [21], the temperature impact on the electrochemical behaviour, battery performance, and ageing was discussed, highlighting that electrochemical-thermal models require internal temperature prediction capability. However, current models (as shown in Table 1 ) are only tested against experimentally measured battery cell surface temperatures. No other study suggests an electrochemical-thermal coupled model which is experimentally validated for core temperature simulation, with the exception of our earlier preliminary study of lumped models [22]. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1, the existing models in their coupled thermal-electrochemical models have not used the newer simplified physics-based model of single particle and the lumped semi-empirical model. To date, no comprehensive thermal-electrochemical simulation study has been conducted that compares the computational cost (time and accuracy), required experimental parameters, and generality of these electrochemical models when combined with a similar thermal model.

The goal of this research is to investigate for the first time the electrochemical-thermal single particle, P2D, and lumped requirements to develop core temperature models for a 21,700-type cell, and to compare the computational requirements and accuracy of each for the generalisation of the results compared to experimental measurements. The LG M50 commercial cell has 267 Wh kg−1 energy (the highest reported in literature) and has gained popularity in the research community [23]. Core temperature measurements (from Yu et al. [24]) and open-circuit voltage data (from Chen et al. [17] for the LG M50 are used (i) to prepare the SP model and adjust the model variables; and (ii) to develop and calibrate the Newman P2D model. For the single particle and Newman P2D models, similar to the work done by Chen et al. [17], use is made of the adjusted variables for 1C-rate discharge measurements. For the single particle and P2D models, the diffusion coefficient of negative electrode was adjusted for different C-rates. The verified and validated models presented here aid in improving the thermal management and design of battery packs, and the design of BMS for aerospace and automotive applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodology: thermal-electrochemical models overview and governing equations
ce:section.ce:para: In the following sections the coupled thermal model with battery models of fully physics-based Newman P2D and the single particle will be described.

Fig. 2 is an integrated battery model (shown schematically) that was created by linking 2D axisymmetric heat transfer and electrochemical cell models. The P2D, single particle, or 0D lumped semi-empirical [22] can be used to simulate the chemistry of the battery cell, while the heat transfer model simulates the internal temperature gradient of the battery. The heat transfer and electrochemical models are connected via heat load determined by the selected electrochemical model as well as the mean temperature determined by the heat transfer model. The following subsections provide an explanation of the electrochemical models' governing equations. The physical properties such as materials and geometry of the cell are used in the 2D heat transfer model, as shown in Fig. 2. A canister, two current collectors, one separator, two electrodes, and an isolator mandrel are all included (it is a common practice that the electrodes and separator are considered as active material). Layers of active material are spirally coiled around the mandrel. These layers typically have a thickness of micrometres and a length of tens of centimetres. In fact, the wound layers are set to be a single domain of active material instead of simulating heat transfer in radial sheets of the wound. The spiral direction of heat conduction in this form of spirally wrapped battery was disregarded (this is suggested in [30]). As a result, axial symmetry can be used. Anisotropic thermal conductivities exist, with stronger heat conductivity in the direction of cylinder length than the radial direction.

The thermal conductivities in the radial and cylinder length directions (taken from [31]), the battery total density, and the thermal capacity (based on [32]) for the active material are calculated in the form of Eqs. (1), (2), (3) and (4) in Table 2 .

In Eqs. (1), (2), (3) and (4),

) and a relationship between the two electrodes in balance. Eqs. (24) and (25) define the electrode host capacities respectively in the global and local formulations, where ∆ SOC is the window of allowed state-of-charge of the electrode material. The positive electrode material is thought to be the source of the cyclable species. The amount of host material in both electrodes varies in lithium-ion battery chemistries. Negative carbon-based electrodes are frequently used more than the positive electrode to minimise metal plating of lithium during charging at high-rate. Due to parasitic interactions, cyclable species might be reduced while the early formation cycles. The generation of the SEI on the graphite is a good example. As a result, the positive and negative electrode host capacities can respectively be obtained by Eqs. (26) and (27), where
Q
cell , 0
is the initial battery cell capacity,
f
cycl , loss
is the fractional loss of cyclable species and
f
host , neg , ex
refers to the proportionate amount by which the negative active host material exceeds the expected or normal level. The initial battery cell capacity,
Q
cell , 0
, can be calculated according to Eq. (28).

The levels of initial concentration in the active species for each electrode establish the battery's initial state of charge. These levels of concentration, however, are not known in advance. An initialization study step for current distribution should be utilised to compute the initial levels of concentration for two electrodes according to the available input parameters. It is possible to specify the initial state-of-charge, individual electrode state-of-charges, or cell voltage.
Q cycl
is the charge of cyclable species and is defined globally (Eq. (29)) and locally (Eq. (30)), where
c
s , avg
is the average species concentration, and
SOC min
is the minimum state of charge allowed in the material of electrode. It should be noted that at the beginning while no gradients of concentration exist within the particles of electrode
c
s , avg
=
c
s , surf
. If the user input is the initial battery state of charge, the species that could be cycled in negative (
Q
cycl , neg
) and positive (
Q
cycl , pos
) electrodes can be obtained using the initial state-of-charge as in Eqs. (31) and (32), where the cell state of charge is 0 ≤
SOC
cell , 0
≤ 1 . If the operational state of charges for individual electrode (i.e.
SOC
Op , pos , 0
and
SOC
Op , neg , 0
) are available, the equations number (33) and (34) apply.

Finally, to simulate the electrochemical energy balance, the single particle model can numerically calculate heat sources (owing to currents). The heat sources can be used in Eq. (5) which represent heat transfer in the active material, the hollow mandrel, and the canister. The heat sources within porous positive and negative electrode (
Q
h , electrode
) caused by the electrochemical process are expressed as Eq. (35), where
E
eq , m
is the equilibrium voltage or the open circuit voltage. The ohmic heating in the electrolyte,
Q
h , sol
, can be calculated using Eq. (36). Therefore, the total volumetric cell heat source is in the form of Eq. (37).

The Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model (also known as P2D model [14]) specifies the current balance in the electrolyte, the lithium salt mass balance, the balances of current in both electrodes, and the lithium mass balance. The dependent variables to be solved are the electrode electric potential,
ϕ s
, the electrolyte potential,
ϕ l
, the potential losses in the porous medium due to SEI, Δ
ϕ s
, the lithium concentration ( Li
Θ s
) in the particles,
c s
, and the salt concentration of electrolyte,
c l
.
c l
represents both lithium cations (
Li +
) and anions (
An −
) concentrations in the electrolyte and pore electrolyte (with an electroneutrality assumption). Table 4 contains the P2D model equations. The equations for binary 1:1 electrolytes in [34] are used to represent the ionic charge balances and material balances.

Using concentrated solution theory, the conservation of current is formulated as Eq. (38), where
σ l
is the electrolyte conductivity, f is the activity coefficient for the salt,
t
Li +
is the transport number for cations (
Li +
),
i tot
is the sum of all electrochemical current sources, and
Q l
is an arbitrary electrolyte current source (usually set to zero). The salt mass balance in the electrolyte is in the form of Eq. (39), where
ε l
denotes the volume fraction of the electrolyte,
D l
is the diffusivity of the electrolyte salt, and
R l
is represents the total cations (
Li +
) source term in the electrolyte. Effective conductivity and diffusivity, that take porosity and tortuosity into account are utilised for the electrolyte phase of porous electrodes.

The conservation of current in the electrode is expressed as Eq. (40), where
Q s
is an arbitrary current source term which is set to zero. Eq. (41) defines the current density in the electrode,
i s
, where
σ s
is the electrode electrical conductivity. As a result, the potential associated with electron conduction,
ϕ s
, is computed via a balance of charge according to Ohm's law, where reactions of charge transfer create either a sink or a source term. Electrochemical insertion reactions take place at spherical particles surface with a radius
r p
in the electrodes. Eq. (42) describes the electrochemical reaction of insertion, where
Θ s
indicates a site of free reaction and
LiΘ s
represents a site of occupied reaction at the surface of particle. The concentration of sites with free reaction can be easily obtained from the Eq. (43), where
c
s , max
is the total or maximum reaction sites concentration which is constant. The solid particles SoC can be calculated using Eq. (44). The SoC is an independent parameter in the functional forms of equilibrium potentials of lithium insertion electrode reactions (
E 0
).

Using Fick's second law of diffusion [33] and similar to the single particle model, Eq. (17) in Table 3 describes the mass balance of lithium in the particles, where
c s
is the concentration of lithium in the solid phase, and
D s
is the solid phase diffusion coefficient. The equations indicate that the reaction occurring on the particle's surface involves the electrode, and lithium moves back and forth between the surface. This equation is solved locally in a 1D pseudo dimension, with the independent variables being the concentrations in the solid phase at the nodal points for the particle's element discretization. It should be noted that in the single particle model, a similar equation can be solved both locally and globally. The boundary condition at the centre is defined as Eq. (18) in Table 3. The boundary conditions at the surface of the spherical particle is shown by Eq. (45) in Table 4, where
R
Li Θ
is the amount of lithium flowing per unit area at the particle surface as a result of electrochemical insertion reactions, expressed in moles (molar flux). Eq. (46) shows a general electrochemical reaction, where
V i
is the coefficient of stoichiometric,
S i
is the system of reactions.
V i
is positive for products in an oxidation reaction (
S ox
) and negative for reduction reaction reactants (
S red
). n is the electrons count (
e −
) and can be calculated by Eq. (47), where
z i
is the charge of species ( i ).

The total current density,
i tot
, in the conservation of current in the electrode, can be obtained in the form of Eq. (48).
A
v , m
is the specific surface which can be calculated based on Eq. (49) considering a spherical shape.

The electrolyte's lithium-ion source term,
R l
, in the salt mass balance (i.e. Eq. (39)) is calculated from Eq. (50), where
R
l , src
is the additional reaction sources (it is set to zero). Based on stoichiometric coefficients of the lithium insertion reaction (i.e.
v
Li +
, m
= − 1 ,
v
An −
, m
= 0 , and
v
LiΘ s
, m
= 1 ), the number of transform electrons using Eq. (47) is
n m
= 1 . Eq. (51) can be used to obtain the source term at the solid particles surface,
R
Li Θ
, that was required in Eq. (45). Eq. (52) can be used to determine the solid phase concentration evolution in time if the diffusion coefficient of solid phase (
D s
, intercalation diffusivity) is reasonably substantial or while the gradients of spatial concentration are ignored, where
R
v Θ
is the negative and positive electrodes molar source. Like in the single particle model,
i
loc , m
is the local intercalation current density (Eq. (22) in Table 3) that can be obtained using Butler-Volmer kinetics [35] at the electrolyte and electrode interface, where
i 0
denotes the exchange current density and can be calculated by Eq. (23) in Table 3.

Eq. (53) calculates the additional internal resistance (film potential loss, ∇
ϕ
s , film
) in the electrodes owing the formation of SEI [34], where
R film
is a generalised film resistance. Eq. (54) calculates the activation overpotential that was required in Eq. (22).

The initial concentration of intercalating species in the material of electrode is known as initial charge distribution study. It should be noted that the cell voltage is determined by solely the disparity in equilibrium potentials between the positive and negative electrode materials since initially there are no applied current and sources of polarization on a battery cell. Two input constraints of the battery cell capacity and voltage can be set.

Eq. (55) calculates the initial cell capacity,
Q
cell , 0
, where
Q
cycl , addm
is the charge of cyclable species in additional porous electrode materials (if present). Eq. (56) determines the charge of cyclable species in an electrode
Q
cycl , electrode
(also an extra electrode material), where
c
s , avg
is the average species concentration and is initially equivalent to the concentration present on the electrode particles' surface (i.e.
c
s , surf
). The second input is cell voltage (Eq. (57)), which is initially equivalent to the variation in the open circuit voltage of the electrode materials. The battery potential is limited to the open circuit voltage, and it must be adjusted as shown in Eq. (58). The intercalated concentration is restricted for any additional electrode material to satisfy Eq. (59). The initial cell state-of-charge,
SOC
cell , 0
, could be also checked during the charge distribution using Eqs. (60) and (61).

Balancing the electrodes in batteries is critical for maximising energy density and life-time, as well as for safety concerns. By balancing the electrodes within the cell, it is ensured that the capacity of the cyclable species can be fully accommodated in either the positive or negative electrode without significant excess material, and that the concentration of intercalating species remains within the designated state-of-charge range. In other words, the quantity of active material in each electrode is determined by the cell's capacity. Like in the single particle model, the electrode host capacities by the initial capacity and the two electrode balancing relation are described respectively in the Eqs. (26) and (27). Eq. (62) can be used to check if the intercalating species concentration remains within the prescribed state-of-charge range (i.e. ∆ SoC ). Therefore, the volume fraction in each electrode,
ε s
, takes the form in Eq. (63) and demonstrates that the capacity needs to be carefully chosen, since capacity is restricted by the size and material of electrode. A limitation on capacity selection is that the volume fraction of electrode cannot be greater than one.

Like the single particle model, heat sources in the negative or positive electrode, the ohmic heating in the electrolyte, and finally the total volumetric cell heat source are obtained using Eqs. (35), (36), and (37).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model requirements: experimental measurements and input parameter values
ce:section.ce:para: Single discharge processes at C rates of 0.3, 0.7 and 1C were simulated to validate the lumped, single particle and DFN models to allow direct comparison to past literature. The computations utilize a commercially available high-energy 21,700 battery rated for 5 Ah of discharge capacity at a 1C rate. Charge and discharge have cut-off voltages which are 2.5 V and 4.2 V. Fig. 3 shows the surface temperature measurements (taken from Yu et al. [24]) serve as the canister's boundary conditions for the heat transfer model. Based on Yu et al. [24], The Maccor cycler is used to conduct measurements with a constant current (CC) discharge at three different C-rates, specifically 0.3, 0.7, and 1. As was shown in Fig. 2, measurements were carried out using fiber optic sensors located both at the surface and core of the cell. Temperature measurements at the surface and core are monitored using Coherent Optical Frequency Domain Reflectometry (C-OFDR), which is a type of distributed fiber optic sensor (DFOS) based on Rayleigh scattering (described by Yu et al. [24]). The Espec thermal chamber is utilised to keep the reference temperature constant at 298.15 K for the instrumented cell, as well as the ambient temperature. The temperature sensor calibration, which involved using a platinum resistance thermometer (PRT) with 0.15 K accuracy, resulted in a standard deviation of 0.27 K [24]. As can be seen in Fig. 3, the higher C-rate generated a higher temperature increase which is a common trend based on the literature. The temperature, for all C-rate curves, plunges close the cut-off (bottom) potential of 2.5 V up to the end of constant current discharge. Full details including images of the experimental setup with which the data was generated are found in [24].

The input variables and the battery geometry characteristics, thermophysical characteristics, and material parameters for the 21,700 NMC cell are included in Table 5 , along with the initial values for the lumped, single particle, and P2D models. For the lumped model, for instance, the ohmic voltage loss initial values at 1C,
η
IR , 1 C
, dimensionless charge exchange current,
J 0
, and time constant of diffusion, τ are calculated based on a sweep test of numerical model. The initial values are utilised in the calibration (data fitting) by putting a least square regression objective on the terminal voltage to calibrate the model. The extra input parameters required for single particle and Newman P2D are distinguished. For the single particle model, the extra parameters are all obtained based on Chen et al. [17]. For the Newman model, the 6 extra parameters are listed at the bottom of Table 5. The parameters were collected from an empirical investigation on the cell parameterization of the LGM50 21,700 5 Ah cell by Warwick Manufacturing Group (WMG), University of Warwick, as the majority of the parameters are not accessible in the user manual for this cell [17]. LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 serves as the positive electrode, and graphite-SiOx serves as the negative electrode. The amount of active material per unit area of the electrode (areal capacity) is reported in a paper by Chen et al. [17]. In a few instances, the values are an approximation of data already available in the literature.
COMSOL Multiphysics® (versions 5.6 and 6), was utilised to conduct simulations. The solutions were numerically carried out by MUMPS solver (time-dependent).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal-electrochemical simulation using the single particle battery model
ce:section.ce:para:
The cell model development was conducted by tuning negative electrode diffusion coefficient for different C-rates to obtain promising cell potential prediction qualities. The tuned parameter values for C-rates 1, 0.7, and 0.3 are respectively
D
s , neg , 1 C
= 5.48 ×
10
− 15
m 2
s
− 1
,
D
s , neg , 0.7 C
= 3.88 ×
10
− 15
m 2
s
− 1
, and
D
s , neg , 0.3 C
= 1.84 ×
10
− 15
m 2
s
− 1
. The rest of the input parameters values are set to 1C discharge experimental values obtained by Chen et al. [17] which are listed in Table 5. Chen et al. [17] have tuned more parameters to obtain the best accuracies for their developed Newman P2D model. Diffusion in the active material particles in the positive and negative electrodes (i.e. Eq. (17)) using diffusion equation (Fick's second law [33]) is solved globally. The gradient in Eq. (17) was calculated in spherical coordinates as the particles were assumed to be best described as spheres (i.e. element order
N ord
= 1 ). Heat of mixing in the porous negative and positive electrodes (due to electrochemical reactions) was included in the cell model (Eq. 35) contributing to the total volumetric cell heat source (Eq. 37). The 2D-axisymetrical heat transfer model shown in Fig. 2, with the temperature boundary conditions shown in Fig. 3, was used in the coupled thermal-electrochemical model.

According to a mesh independence test with total grid counts of 48, 68, 105, 174, 468, and 864, Table 6 compares the maximum temperatures at various C-rates (together with their related tuning parameters). The associated total number of DOFs (
N DOF
) solved are included. Furthermore, to assess the gird sensitivity of the single particle model considering the 1D pseudo extra dimension, several numbers of elements were set (i.e.
N el
= 5 , 10 , and
20 ). The maximum grid sensitivity to the number of elements in the discretized particles is negligible (0.6 % when
N el
is varied from 10 to 20 for the coarse heat transfer mesh count of 48 at 0.7C). For 1 and 0.3C-rates, the difference in maximum temperature between number of meshes of 48 and 68 is small, supporting the coupled model's mesh independence. However, the difference in maximum temperature between number of meshes of 68 and 105 is insignificant at 0.7C-rate. In order to achieve a maximum absolute change of temperature smaller than 0.1 %, the lowest count of mesh with the converged element count was chosen. It is notable that for all three of the C-rates examined, the mesh count of 174 meets this requirement.

Figs. 4.a, 5.a, and 6.a
show the predicted cell potentials at 1C, 0.7C, and 0.3C against the corresponding experimental cell voltages (respectively shown in green and red solid lines). At each C-rates, the associated experimental cell open circuit voltage has been used as an input to the battery model (shown in blue solid line).

Figs. 4.b, 5.b, and 6.b show the validation results for the core temperature computations respectively at 1, 0.7, and 0.3C-rates. The calibrated model with the 1C discharge experimental data input and the associated tuning parameter (i.e.
D
s , neg
) at each C-rate was successful in the calculation of temperature and load curves, which offers a good level of generalisation. As was expected, the standard deviations for the single particle model at 1C in Table 7 are all lower than those for the lumped semi-empirical model in [22], showing a better accuracy for a physics-based model. However, owing to the less generalisation of the single particle model for lower C-rates, the lumped semi-empirical model shows better standard deviations at 0.7 and 0.3C-rates. This is mainly due to the lumped model's strong generalisation.

When comparing the SP model to the existing models in the literature, the errors (RMS) of Chen et al. [17] are lower which is owing to using a high-fidelity DFN model as well as tuning more input parameters (P2D RMSE: 46 mV). Computational time of a Newman P2D model is longer than the simplified single particle model. In the study by O'Regan et al. [23], it is stated that the computations take 10–20 min (2–75 times more than the single particle model computations in Table 7). In addition, O'Regan et al. [23] reported that the achieved battery potential RMSE is 140 mV when the parameter set of Chen et al. [17] is used in their model. They were able to achieve an RMSE of 100 mV by further tuning the input parameter set. The worst RMSE for the present single particle model is 145 mV. As was mentioned before, for their projections of surface temperature, O'Regan et al. [23] have not included the standard deviations or RMSE. Furthermore, there is no core temperature simulation model in the literature making comparisons impossible. By inspecting the provided results, however, the surface (not core) temperature standard deviations in [23] look identical to the single particle standard deviations as in Table 7. Table 7 shows standard deviations decrease from 1C to 0.3C. The smaller gradients of temperature in the measurements and the better resolution (i.e., more data points) for the lower C-rate values are the causes of these decreases. The tuned single particle model offers a high prediction accuracy and a relatively fast computational time (21 s and 31 s at mesh counts of 68 and 105) in comparison with the existing models. Therefore, this model can be applied to future model-based thermal management systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal-electrochemical simulation using the Newman P2D (DFN) battery model
ce:section.ce:para:
The 1D geometry, materials, and boundary conditions of the DFN model are schematically shown in Fig. 7 . A voltage of 0 V is applied on the negative electrode's current collector boundary to ground it (

). Graphite-SiOx material is used for the negative electrode, and LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC 811) is used for the positive electrode. The electrolyte is made up of 1000 mol/m3 LiPF6 salt in a solvent of 3:7 (by volume or V:V) ethylene carbonate (EC):ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC). This is similar electrolyte examined in Nyman et al. [36], hence the parameters established in that study were used in the simulation.

The electrolyte conductivity and the equilibrium potential of the negative and positive electrodes are composition dependent through experimentally collected data. For electrolyte conductivity, this data is reported in interpolating functions with respect to salt content in the electrolyte (

For the charge distribution study and balancing purposes, the initial concentrations for positive and negative electrodes are respectively set to 13,975 mol/m3 and 28,866 mol/m3 (this is achieved by trial and error and is taken from [23]). Initially, for the voltage profile of 1C case, positive and negative electrode diffusivities were manually adjusted to get satisfactory agreement between the simulated and experimental results. The chosen positive electrode diffusivity is
D
s , pos , 1 C
= 4 ×
10
− 15
, and the negative electrode diffusivity is
D
s , neg , 1 C
= 1.74 ×
10
− 14
m 2
s
− 1
, which are respectively 170 % and 900 % different to the experimental values reported in [17,23]. However, in both previous works of [17,23] the chosen positive and negative electrode diffusivities for the simulations deviate by 170 % and 1797 % respectively from the experimental values. The reported experimental values are
D
s , pos , 1 C
= 1.48 ×
10
− 15
m 2
s
− 1
and
D
s , neg , 1 C
= 1.74 ×
10
− 15
m 2
s
− 1
. Therefore, in the current study, the adjusted negative electrode diffusivity is closer to the experimental results when compared with the previous models. For the voltage profiles of 0.7C-rate and 0.3C-rate the negative electrode diffusivity needs to be further adjusted to achieve a promising prediction quality, particularly, at the lower SOCs (or at the end of the voltage curves) as well as relaxation (which is when the current is set to zero). Other parameters remain equal to parameterisation of 1C simulation. It should be noted that this was not possible with the single particle model, demonstrating that the P2D model is more general. The negative electrode diffusivity adjustments for the prediction of 0.7C (
D
s , neg , 0.7 C
= 1.34 ×
10
− 14
m 2
s
− 1
) and 0.3C (
D
s , neg , 0.3 C
= 0.94 ×
10
− 14
m 2
s
− 1
) voltage profiles are relatively minor in comparison with those reported in the literature which offers a better generalisation. The mesh independency study for the thermal-electrochemical model was conducted by taking into account the particle discretisation in pseudo dimension and number of elements in the 1D mesh used for DFN model (i.e. the electrochemical model) as well as the 2D mesh in the thermal model. Like in single particle model, the lowest count of mesh that produced a maximum absolute change in temperature of smaller than 0.1 % is what was used to determine the converged element count. It was found that changing the number of elements used for the particle discretisation in pseudo dimension from 5 to 10, and from 10 to 20, gives a maximum absolute temperature-change of <0.1 %. Thus, the number of elements for the particle discretisation in the pseudo dimension has been set to 20 for all the results.

Table 8 shows the findings of the mesh independence investigation comparing the highest temperatures at various discharge C-rates (together with their associated tuning parameters) with total counts of grid of 48, 68, and 105. Changing the grid numbers in the 2D-axisymetrical mesh used for the heat transfer model from 48 to 68, 105, 175, 468, and 864 resulted in an absolute change in temperature of smaller than 0.1 %, meeting the independency criterion. Table 8 reports only grid numbers up to 105 for the sake of simplicity. The total number of DOFs solved (
N DOF
) is provided. Furthermore, several numbers of elements were established to examine the grid sensitivity of the P2D model while considering the cell's 1D dimension (i.e.
N
el , 1 D
= 28, 50, and 100). Changing the number of elements in the 1D mesh used for DFN model from 28 to 50 and from 50 to 100 also resulted a maximum absolute change in temperature of smaller than 0.1 %, hence meeting the independency criterion. As a result, when compared to the lumped (in [22]) and single particle models, the DFN model is less mesh dependent when coupled with the heat transfer model; however, the computational time is prolonged. Mesh independence was achieved for heat transfer model grid numbers of 468 and 105 using the lumped model and the single particle models in the thermal-electrochemical coupled models.

Table 9 shows the standard deviations of potential and temperature, and computing time, for all counts of mesh and three numbers of elements (
N
el , 1 D
) in the 1D electrochemical model. Each simulation takes between 27 and 52 s on a 2.90GHz CPU (Intel® Xeron® Gold 6226R) with a RAM of 64 GB, depending on the mesh counts. When used as thermal-electrochemical models, the computational time for the DFN model is between 22 s and 34 s longer than the lumped model ([22]) and between 11 s and 21 s longer than the single particle model, which is due to the difference between 0D and 1D modelling and the extra parameterisation required. This represents a 189 % increase over the lumped model computation time. As predicted, the P2D model does not provide the same generality as the lumped semi-empirical model, which is comparable to the single particle in the previous section and prior research of physics-based models in the literature.

Figs. 8.a, 9.a, and 10.a
compare predicted cell potentials at 1C, 0.7C, and 0.3C to experimental cell voltages (respectively shown in green and red solid lines). Using Eqs. (64) and (65), the positive and negative cell open circuit voltages at each C-rate were calculated and input into the battery model (shown in blue solid line). Figs. 8.b, 9.b, and 10.b illustrate the validation results for the temperature calculations at 1, 0.7, and 0.3C-rates, respectively. As shown, the developed (fitted) model with the 1C discharge experimental data input and the associated tuning parameters (i.e.
D
s , neg
) at each C-rate was successful in predicting load and temperature curves, indicating a good level of generalisation. The cell voltage standard deviations for the P2D model at three C-rates in Table 9 are all lower than those for the lumped semi-empirical model in [22] and single particle model in Table 7, indicating that a fully physics-based model has better accuracy. However, because the P2D and single particle models are less general for lower C-rates, the lumped semi-empirical model [22] has better core temperature standard deviations at 0.7 and 0.3C-rates. This is primarily due to the strong generalisation of the lumped model when coupled with the heat transfer model [22]. The current P2D model has the worst RMS error of 44 mV. When comparing the current P2D model to the existing models in the literature, Chen et al. [17] (P2D RMSE: 46 mV) and O'Regan et al. [23] (P2D RMSE: 100 mV) report worse RMS errors. The simulations in the study by O'Regan et al. [23] take 10 to 20 min (21 to 43 times longer than the present P2D computations in Table 9).

Examining the relaxation process (i.e. when the current load is set to zero) at the end of the discharge process in subplots of Figs. 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, as well as lumped model results in [22], it is clear that when coupled with the heat transfer model, the DFN and lumped models are more accurate in predicting both the battery voltage profile and the temperature results. This implies that these models may perform better in a full charge-discharge process, which is desirable in a thermal management model. Furthermore, because the DFN model solves electrode dynamics in 1D rather than 0D for lumped and single particle models, results associated with the length of the battery cell model are accessible, which can be advantageous in the design processes. For instance, total dissipation power density in the length of the battery cell model at different times during the discharge process is plotted in Fig. 11 for three C-rates (red lines with diamond markers are the results around maximum core temperature). It should be noted that the variations along the length of the battery cell are caused by differences in the calculated electrode and electrolyte current densities and potentials, all of which are accessible in the DFN model. Other useful examples include the concentrations of solid lithium and electrolyte salt along the length of the cell, which affects the relative change in volume, stress, and strain, all of which are affected by the solid phase concentrations, the figures for which are beyond the scope of this study. As a result, when a high-fidelity model is required, particularly for state of health or fire safety purposes during the safety phase of a disruptive battery management and thermal management system, a P2D model can be very useful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model choice based on different quality factors and their superiority over one another
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, choosing an electrochemical model is a trade-off between computational time, accuracy, generality, high fidelity, and the amount of experimental parametrisation required. Table 10 in this study shows the superiority of each model choice when combined with a similar heat transfer model. Table 10 employs a first, second, and third ranking system, with the first representing the best and the third representing the worst. To make the decision, all the values (e.g., first has a value of 1) for each model are added up, and the model with the lowest summation is ranked highest. To make more room for inspection, the lumped model results in [22] are also considered. As can be seen, the semi-empirical lumped model takes first place in four categories, placing it first overall. This is because it has the shortest computational time, the highest accuracy in predicting maximum core temperature, the best generalisation over three power rates (C-rates), and the fewest required input parameters. The P2D (DFN) and single particle models respectively receive second and third places. The P2D model has the highest electrochemical accuracy and, in particular, better generality (when compared to the single particle), which may be useful in saving time during the model calibration process. However, the decision should be based solely on the modelling requirements of the battery management and thermal management systems. One may choose the P2D model because of its superiority in predicting several thermophysical characteristics in the length of the electrodes and separator, whereas another may argue that only the computational cost (both time and accuracy) is important in the thermal management of battery packs with different topologies (i.e. individual cells at the module scale) in electric vehicles, and thus choose the lumped or single particle model. The current findings may be of great interest to researchers in the battery and thermal management communities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Full cycle (discharge and charge) generalisation tests on lumped model simulations
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, now that it has been shown that the lumped model offers an incredible potential (ranked first by quality checks of Table 10), a severe generalisation test of this model will be conducted. In this regard, for the first time, a set of data with full discharge and charge cycle has been used to test the generalisation, accuracy, and suitability of this model for real-time parameterisation cases. Use is made of a lumped model that has been only calibrated for 1C-rate constant current discharge experimental results in our previous work [22]. The experimental data are taken from [24], and include a subsequent constant current discharge and constant current constant voltage (CCCV) charge at 1C, 0.7C, and 0.3C rates. For the follow up CCCV charge, the cut-off voltage and current are respectfully set at 4.2 V and 0.25A.

As can be seen from the Figs. 12, 13, and 14
, the model is successful in predicting the associated full-cycle loads and core temperatures at different c-rates of 1C, 0.7C, and 0.3C. The standard deviations (SD) of voltage and core temperature are listed in Table 11 . This table allows for inspecting the differences in the SDs of a full cycle and a discharge cycle. As can be seen, for all the full cycles, the cell voltage SDs are improved as a result of constant voltage charge process towards the end of curves. However, as it was expected, the core temperature SDs are increased. The SD percentage differences at 1C, 0.7C, and 0.3C are respectively 21 %, 17 %, and 2 %. This means that the generalised model for the full cycle is still fairly accurate. Overall, this is a very impressive level of generalisation when compared with other equivalent circuit or lumped models in the literature. For instance, those by Li et al. [37] that are individually calibrated for different C-rates and only for discharge. There is no report of generalisation study in [37]. Further inspection in Figs. 12.a, 13.a, and 14.a, reveals that the model, however, is not as accurate as P2D and SP models in this study at the bottom state of charge levels. This can be explained by exploring different voltage loss contributors in the calculation of terminal voltages (Eq. (69)) by lumped model. These ohmic, activation and concentration overpotential contributors are listed briefly in Table 12 . These equations are taken from our previous work (an in-detail explanation of the governing equations of this model are in [22]). As shown in Figs. 12.c, 13.c, and 14.c, it seems that the two first contributors of ohmic and overpotential are constant during different constant current discharge and charge but are nonlinear at the constant voltage charge processes (note that ohmic overpotential Eq. (70) is linearly related to current but activation voltage loss contributor (72) is in fact not a constant). Therefore, the overprediction of voltage at the highest depth of discharge (near to bottom 2.5 V cut-off) is governed by the concentration overpotential (Eq. (73)), which is diffusion related. Table 12 shows that diffusion Eq. (74) is a first order ordinary differential equation (ODE). Thus, a higher order diffusion equation in the lumped model or other reduced order models in the literature could address this overprediction that is out of the scope of this study. In real-time parameterisation, to save computational load, a combination of lumped and another model (e.g. SP, P2D or EC, depending on the required accuracy) could be used in a way that in lower SOC ranges near the bottom voltage of 2.5 V, the model switches for a short to another model insuring the accurate prediction of bottom cut-offs. This can be easily implemented by a fuzzy logic, gain scheduling PID, or Fuzzy-PID controllers which are common practice in the transport, genset and other energy storage sectors using lithium-ion batteries.

For the first time, numerical simulations were used to evaluate the electrochemical-thermal model requirements of a high energy-density 21,700 NMC commercial battery with capabilities of core temperature estimations. First, a single particle model and a Newman P2D (DFN) fully-physics-based model are connected to a 2D axisymmetric heat transfer model to accomplish this. For calibration and validation of the models, an experimental database including a variety of C-rates (0.3C, 0.7C, and 1C) during constant current discharge of the cell was employed. A fiber sensing sensor is installed in the cell to detect the core and surface temperatures. The measurements for canister surface temperature serve as heat transfer boundary conditions, while the experimental open circuit potential data are correlated depending on charge state and utilised as inputs to the electrochemical models. For these electrochemical models, the calibration, mesh independence, and standard deviations for battery potentials and core temperatures, also the computing time, are completely described and compared with a previously developed lumped model.

For the single particle model, the negative and positive electrode diffusivities are adjusted to curve fit the model via the 1C voltage profile. Then the negative electrode diffusivity is further adjusted to achieve good fits to the 0.3C and 0.7C potential profiles. The calibrated model with the 1C discharge experimental data input and the associated tuning parameter (i.e.
D
s , neg
) at each C-rate simulated the load and temperature curves successfully. The core temperature standard deviations for the single particle model at 1C are lower than those for the lumped model, however, the lumped semi-empirical model shows better standard deviations at 0.7 and 0.3C-rates. Depending on the counts of mesh, each computation takes 16 to 262 s. This is 1356 % increase in comparison with the previously developed lumped model computation time (i.e. maximum 18 s). The single particle model does not provide the same generalisation as the lumped model, which is consistent with previous studies of P2D models in the literature.

For the P2D model, for the voltage profile of 1C-rate, positive and negative electrode diffusivities were manually adjusted to achieve satisfactory agreement. For 0.7C-rate and 0.3C-rate voltage profiles, the negative electrode diffusivity can be kept constant at the expense of lower accuracy at the end of discharge and relaxation. This was not possible with a single particle model, showing that the P2D model is more general. Furthermore, when compared to the P2D models in the literature, the adjusted negative electrode diffusivity is closer to the experimental results. Each simulation takes between 27 and 52 s, representing a 189 % increase over the time required to compute the lumped model in a previous study. The literature P2D simulations take between 21 and 43 times longer than the presented P2D model simulations in this paper. When compared to the lumped model, the single particle model, and existing models in the literature, the presented P2D model has a lower RMS error of 44 mV. In a quality check for finding the best model, five qualities of computational time, accuracy, generality, high fidelity, and required parameterisation were taken into account. Lumped model overall ranked first, followed by P2D and Single particle standing second and third. Then, the lumped model that was calibrated for a 1C-discharge were put under a severe generalisation test to predict for the first time a full discharge-charge cycle at different C-rates. The results showed that the lumped model is incredibly capable of accurately being generalised to such full cycle experimental database. The standard deviations were only slightly greater than those for a half cycle validation test in a previous study of the same authors. The results from this work can be used in future by enabling a generalised lumped model to be confidently used in real-time battery management and energy and thermal management systems of mobility or stationary energy storage system sectors.

Alireza Sarmadian: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Methodology, Data Curation, Validation, Visualization, Writing- original draft. W. Dhammika Widanage: Methodology, Formal analysis. Barbara Shollock: Investigation, Project Management, Funding acquisition. Francesco Restuccia: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing- original draft, Writing- review & editing, Methodology, Supervision.
